video_title: Euron Greyjoy's apocalypse in the Game of Thrones books
claim: Euron Greyjoy is secretly pursuing godlike/eldritch power through blood magic and a possible connection to Bloodraven's greenseeing, and will use it -- potentially via the Horn of Winter at Oldtown -- to unleash a White Walker apocalypse, fulfilling a cyclical repeat of the ancient Bloodstone Emperor who caused the first Long Night.
sub_claims: Euron secretly hired a Faceless Man assassin, paid with a dragon egg, to murder Balon Greyjoy and claim the Iron Islands' throne.

As a boy, Euron was visited in a dream by Bloodraven (the three-eyed crow), who opened his 'third eye' to magic -- paralleling Bran Stark's own awakening -- even though Bloodraven ultimately rejected him as a student.
Euron secretly possesses greenseer-derived magic powers such as skinchanging and dream-walking.
Euron is orchestrating a mass blood-magic sacrifice (depicted in the unpublished 'Forsaken' chapter) to transform himself into a godlike, monstrous being.
Euron intends to seize the true Horn of Winter (secretly in Sam Tarly's possession at Oldtown) to bring down the Wall and unleash the White Walkers in a new Long Night.
Euron is a cyclical repeat/parallel of the ancient Bloodstone Emperor, the legendary figure whose blasphemy and blood magic caused the original Long Night.
asx_verdict: Presented largely as a speculative synthesis of fan theories (heavily crediting theorist Poor Quentyn) built on textual hints -- the video repeatedly flags pieces as 'theory', 'speculation', or 'maybe' rather than settled fact, while treating Euron's blood-magic ritual and villain characterization as strongly text-supported.
notes: Chains at least 6 distinct sub-theories (Balon's murder via Faceless Men, Bloodraven/greenseer origin, skinchanging powers, the Forsaken blood ritual, Horn of Winter/White Walker apocalypse, Bloodstone Emperor parallel) into one cumulative 'Euron brings the apocalypse' narrative. Draws heavily on the unpublished TWOW 'Forsaken' sample chapter (read by GRRM at a convention) for its most vivid evidence -- flagged source_domain unknown, outside our 5-book corpus. Also includes non-falsifiable literary/thematic argument (the 'silence' motif vs. the series title, the Feast for Crows title reading) that cannot re-ground against plot evidence. Unlike a contested-theory video (e.g. R+L=J), this one is mostly single-direction theory-building -- explicit counter-evidence/objections are rare (only 2 beats), the video's own hedges ('speculation', 'maybe', 'it's just a theory') do the doubt-flagging instead.

paraphrase: Euron possesses mysterious magical trappings -- the horn Dragonbinder, warlock 'slaves', and Valyrian steel armour -- linking him to Bloodraven, the Faceless Men, and Valyria.
Euron's ship the Silence has a mute crew with their tongues ripped out, black sails, and decks painted red to hide blood; it is feared all across the eastern seas from Ib to Asshai.
Victarion and Aeron hate and fear Euron even before he appears in the story; Aeron thinks of him in ominous terms.
spoken_quote: and its name is Euron Crow's Eye

paraphrase: In Book 3, King Balon dies suddenly, falling from a bridge on Pyke; Euron returns the very next day after years away and claims the throne. Asha thinks Euron murdered Balon.

Arya meets the witch called the ghost of High Heart, who has a vision of a faceless man on a swaying bridge with a drowned, seaweed-hung crow perched on his shoulder -- read as a Faceless Man assassin marked with Euron's symbol.
spoken_quote: a drowned crow with seaweed hanging from his wings

paraphrase: It's implied that the Faceless Men can be paid with a meaningful personal sacrifice instead of money, especially for an important target.
Euron claims he once had a dragon egg, tried to make it hatch, got frustrated, and threw it into the sea.
Some fans believe Euron actually gave the dragon egg to the Faceless Men as payment for killing Balon, rather than really discarding it.

Euron's eyepatch symbolizes his hidden nature: his visible 'smiling eye' is charming and magnanimous, while the hidden eye behind the patch is his 'blood eye', where his cruelty, sorcery, and ambition live.
spoken_quote: blood eye

paraphrase: At the kingsmoot, Euron wins the crown through a mix of bribery, murder, and grandiose boasting about raiding, raping, and looting -- exactly the qualifications the ironborn respect -- while promising to restore their glorious past as conquerors.

Euron shows off his magic horn Dragonbinder at the kingsmoot, claiming it can bind dragons to his will, and announces a plan to sail to Meereen to steal Daenerys Targaryen's dragons and use them to conquer Westeros.
Euron declares he wants 'the world', wants dragons and 'Woe', and plans to marry Daenerys.
spoken_quote: When the kraken weds the dragon

paraphrase: Euron sends his brother Victarion, who hates him (Euron slept with Victarion's wife, leading Victarion to beat her to death, which caused Euron's exile), east with Dragonbinder to fetch Daenerys; Victarion secretly plans to take her for himself as revenge.
Euron knows about and exploits Victarion's resentment to manipulate him -- Victarion is described as a puppet, blind to the forces controlling him.
spoken_quote: blind to the tentacles that grasp

paraphrase: Dragonbinder is said to bring dragons to the horn's true 'master', not whoever blows it -- and Euron, not Victarion, is that master, implying Victarion is doomed to fail at capturing a dragon for himself.
Fan speculation that Euron might ultimately steal the dragon Viserion himself, similar to how the Night King steals a dragon in the TV show.
Euron says that as a boy he dreamed he could fly; when he woke, his maester told him he couldn't, but Euron questions whether the maester lied.
spoken_quote: know unless we leap from some tall tower?

paraphrase: This mirrors Bran Stark's story: Bran falls from a tower, then dreams of flying and is visited in the dream by the three-eyed crow (Bloodraven), who opens his third eye to magic; Bran's maester likewise says he can't do magic, and is wrong.
Bloodraven is called the 'three-eyed crow' and has one red eye; Euron is called the 'Crow's Eye' and his sigil is one red eye with crows -- a naming and symbol parallel.
Theory: Euron, like Bran, was visited in his dreams by the three-eyed crow (Bloodraven).

In Bran's vision he sees the remains of many other dreamers Bloodraven tried to reach, suggesting Bloodraven auditioned many potential students who failed or died; theorized that Euron may have been one of these rejected students -- seen as too cruel to teach, but left with his third eye opened regardless.
spoken_quote: the bones of a thousand other dreamers

paraphrase: Bloodraven and Bran are greenseers with the power to skinchange into other creatures' minds; it's unconfirmed whether Euron has this power, but the video argues it would make sense given the dream parallel.
Euron gives Victarion a mute 'dusky woman' companion for the eastward voyage; fans speculate she is spying for Euron, possibly via Euron skinchanging into her from afar.
Skinchanging a human is treated as an evil, abominable act in-world -- consistent with something Euron, who breaks every taboo, might be willing to do.
Skinchanging could explain how Euron coordinates his mute crew aboard the Silence, since the crew cannot speak to receive normal orders.
Some ironborn are said to be able to skinchange into sea creatures, showing the ability isn't exclusive to Starks/wargs -- supporting the idea that Euron could be a skinchanger too.

After Euron announces he'll marry Daenerys, she has an unsettling sex dream of a man with blue, bruised lips; Euron's own lips are blue and bruised from a drug he takes, implying he magically entered her dream.
Euron also enters Aeron's dreams later in the story, another instance of apparent dream-walking magic.

In Bran's flying dream he sees the whole world spread out below him -- Winterfell, Asshai, dragons, and the heart of winter -- and it's theorized Euron may have seen the same things in his own dream, explaining his hunger for total power.
spoken_quote: The whole world

paraphrase: Euron says he sailed to Asshai by the Shadow, at the edge of the map, and saw wonders and terrors beyond imagining.
spoken_quote: wonders and terrors beyond imagining

paraphrase: Many of the story's most powerful sorcerers (Melisandre, Quaithe, Marwyn, Mirri Maz Duur) learned their magic in Asshai, framing it as a hub of dark power that Euron also drew from.
Near Asshai lies Stygai, the 'corpse city', feared even by shadowbinders; it's speculated Euron may have dared to enter it and found some evil power there.

Euron says he sailed to Valyria, the smoking demon-haunted ruins where it's said no one can survive -- and he does possess Valyrian steel armour and a Valyrian horn (Dragonbinder), suggesting he gained power there.
The old Valyrian empire used dragons and slavery to conquer the world, which may have inspired Euron's own ambitions of conquest.

Euron captured warlocks from Qarth (recently defeated by Daenerys) and took their 'shade of the evening', a psychedelic drug that gives visions of past and future and stains his lips blue and bruised.
Euron says the shade 'opens his eyes' and that there's more truth in it than in all the gods of earth, reflecting his belief that gods and morality are lies.
spoken_quote: more truth in it than all the gods of earth

paraphrase: Euron has murdered three of his own brothers -- Balon, and Harlon and Robin Greyjoy when they were young -- with kinslaying regarded as the ironborn's most abhorrent crime.

After choking the life from Harlon, Euron pissed into the sea and waited for a god to strike him down; none did, leading him to conclude that gods are lies and men are meat, feeling godlike for breaking every taboo with impunity.
spoken_quote: None did

paraphrase: Aeron's terror of Euron stems from childhood sexual abuse -- Euron says he 'taught Aeron how to pray', since Aeron would pray in fear whenever Euron came to 'visit' him at night.
spoken_quote: visit

paraphrase: Euron learned that sadistic cruelty inspires worship -- when he terrorizes the eastern seas aboard the Silence, men pray, and he calls himself the godliest man ever to raise sail.
spoken_quote: the godliest man ever to raise sail

paraphrase: In the unreleased 'Forsaken' chapter (from the upcoming Winds of Winter, read publicly by George R. R. Martin at a convention), Euron imprisons and tortures Aeron physically and psychologically, forcing him to drink shade of the evening, which gives Aeron nightmarish visions of dead brothers and dead gods.
book_hint: TWOW Forsaken sample chapter (read by GRRM at a convention)

paraphrase: In the Forsaken chapter, Euron declares that these are the last days, when the world shall be broken and remade, and that a new god shall be born from the graves and charnel pits.
spoken_quote: These are the last days, when the world shall be broken and remade. A new god shall be born from the graves and charnel pits

paraphrase: Euron's hidden 'blood eye' is finally revealed in full horror, and he proclaims himself both king and god.
spoken_quote: I am your god

paraphrase: Euron imprisons priests of multiple religions -- priests of the Seven with their tongues ripped out, a red priest with a burned-off face, a warlock whose legs he rips off -- plus a girl named Falia, whom he seduced and impregnated with a promise of marriage before ripping out her tongue.
Euron ties Falia, Aeron, and the imprisoned priests to the front of his ships as he sails into battle against an enemy fleet, setting up a mass blood sacrifice.

The coming battle is set to kill the imprisoned priests, Falia and her unborn child, Aeron, the enemy fleet, and Euron's own fleet in a catastrophic orgy of death; Aeron dreams of ironborn ships in the middle of it.
spoken_quote: burning on a boiling blood-red sea

paraphrase: This mass-death ritual might 'summon krakens from the deep' -- in Aeron's vision krakens come at Euron's command, and it's established that krakens can be drawn to the surface by blood, matching the vision of a blood-red sea.
The red priest Moqorro has a vision of Euron transformed into a monstrous being with one black eye and many long arms, sailing on a sea of blood.
spoken_quote: A tall and twisted thing with one black eye and ten long arms, sailing on a sea of blood

paraphrase: Aeron sees Euron as no longer human, more squid than man, his face a mass of writhing tentacles.
spoken_quote: his face a mass of writhing tentacles

paraphrase: Speculation that the Drowned God the ironborn worship might actually be an eldritch monster that Euron's blood sacrifice will awaken.
Alternative speculation that the dragon egg Euron threw into the sea earlier might hatch into a monstrous sea dragon as a result of all the blood magic.
Euron and his ironborn have been raiding towards Oldtown, and it's hinted his coming power will be turned against the city.
Melisandre has a vision of towers by the sea, submerged beneath a black and bloody tide, rising from the depths -- read as Oldtown's Hightower under threat.
spoken_quote: towers by the sea, submerged beneath a black and bloody tide

paraphrase: Oldtown's Hightower is built on an ancient black-stone foundation which some legends say was built by fish-people ('Deep Ones'), linking it to Lovecraftian mythology.
The Citadel in Oldtown holds magical Valyrian artefacts called glass candles.
A Faceless Man has infiltrated the Citadel, possibly searching for a blood-soaked tome called 'The Death of Dragons'.
Oldtown is ruled by Leyton Hightower and his secluded 'Mad Maid', who have stayed in the Hightower for ten years consulting books of spells, possibly to raise an army from the deep.
spoken_quote: raise an army from the deep

paraphrase: Legends tell of a Horn of Winter with the power to bring down the Wall; Mance Rayder claimed to have it, but it turned out to be fake.

In Book 2, Jon finds an old horn and gives it to Sam, who takes it to Oldtown; neither thinks it's important, but the text keeps subtly reminding readers Sam still has it, leading fans to suspect it is secretly the true Horn of Winter.
Speculation that Euron wants a White Walker apocalypse, since he says he wants to break the world and the Forsaken chapter is full of apocalyptic imagery.
Euron may have learned about the White Walkers during his childhood flying dream, paralleling how Bran's dream shows him the heart of winter at the end of the world.
spoken_quote: the heart of winter

paraphrase: Legend holds that the White Walkers will bring a Long Night that never ends.
Euron is repeatedly described with imagery of night and darkness -- hair black as a midnight sea, sails black as a starless sky -- and says the ironborn shall feast before the fall of night.
spoken_quote: we shall feast before the fall of night

paraphrase: At the kingsmoot, Euron's horn sounds three times; at the Night's Watch, three horn blasts is the traditional signal that White Walkers are coming.
Eastern legend holds that the first Long Night was caused by the Bloodstone Emperor.
The Bloodstone Emperor murdered his older sibling, the Amethyst Empress, to take her throne -- paralleling Euron murdering Balon to take his throne.
The Bloodstone Emperor practiced dark arts, torture, slavery, and cannibalism -- paralleling Euron's own practice of dark arts, torture, slavery, and cannibalism.
The Bloodstone Emperor 'cast down the true gods', paralleling how Euron mocks and attacks the gods.
spoken_quote: cast down the true gods

paraphrase: The Bloodstone Emperor worshipped a sinister black stone, paralleling the black stone of the ironborn throne, the Hightower's foundation, and the city of Asshai.
The Bloodstone Emperor married a 'tiger-woman', paralleling Euron's ambition to marry the 'dragon-woman' Daenerys.
The sister the Bloodstone Emperor usurped was called the Amethyst Empress; Euron himself points out that Daenerys's eyes are amethysts, a symbolic linguistic link.
spoken_quote: eyes are amethysts

paraphrase: Euron's hidden eye is called his 'blood eye', thematically echoing the name Bloodstone Emperor.
Rodrik the Reader states that history is a wheel and that what has happened before will happen again, framing Euron as history repeating the Bloodstone Emperor's role.
spoken_quote: history is a wheel

paraphrase: Fan theorist Poor Quentyn's theory: Euron will climb the Hightower in Oldtown and sound the Horn of Winter, breaking the Wall, bringing the Long Night, and sparking the apocalypse.

Thematic argument: Euron embodies 'silence', the opposite of the series' title A Song of Ice and Fire -- his ship is named Silence, he mutes his crew, and he silences his brothers through trauma; a sudden silence falls when he first enters the story.

Thematic argument: A Feast for Crows depicts only opportunists (the Bloody Mummers, Lady Stoneheart, Littlefinger, the Boltons, and Euron) benefiting from the war's chaos, with Euron declaring Westeros is dying and that his followers shall feast -- framing him as the title's literal 'Crow'.
spoken_quote: Those who follow me shall feast until the end of their days

paraphrase: Euron never personally swings a sword or casts a spell on-page -- his power comes entirely from exploiting people's fear, hope, and greed; he was chosen and 'democratically elected' at the kingsmoot.

The video raises its own doubt about Euron's claim to have simply thrown the dragon egg away, noting dragon eggs are extremely valuable and rare -- casting doubt on Euron's stated version before offering the Faceless-Men-payment alternative.

The video explicitly flags uncertainty about the Forsaken chapter's monstrous/eldritch imagery, saying it's 'not clear what exactly will happen' -- treating the Drowned-God-awakening and egg-hatches-into-a-sea-dragon ideas as open speculation rather than confirmed outcomes.
spoken_quote: not clear what exactly will happen
